explanation: This is a simple infographic showing a world map with 5 color-coded continents and their corresponding population numbers. The design is clean with minimal elements, large readable text, ample white space, and only a single variable (nationality distribution) displayed. The visual encoding is straightforward with no dense data or multiple layers of information.

This is a grouped bar chart with two categories (Refinance and Home purchase) over 12 years, totaling about 24 bars. While it has more elements than a simple bar chart, the design is clean with clear color encoding (green and orange), readable labels, and informative annotations. The moderate number of data points and dual-category grouping push it slightly above a simple bar chart but it remains straightforward to interpret.

This is a horizontal stacked bar chart with 19 risk factor categories and 3 income-level color encodings, resulting in roughly 57 data segments. The numerous labeled categories and stacked structure add moderate complexity, though the consistent layout and clear legend keep it reasonably interpretable.

This is a horizontal stacked/grouped bar chart with 20 countries and two color-encoded categories (Deceased and Living donors). While the chart uses a straightforward design with clear labels, the relatively large number of countries and dual-bar encoding creates moderate visual density and comparison demands, pushing it slightly above a simple bar chart.

This is a standard scatterplot with approximately 16 labeled data points encoded by 4 color categories (Dense, Octree, JIT, Opt.). While the individual labels add some visual clutter, especially in the crowded lower-right region, the chart uses a single panel with straightforward axes, keeping overall complexity moderate.
Anchor image scored at 0.54.

This is a stacked area chart with 8 country layers over 12 time points, using a brown/tan color palette. The multiple overlapping layers and legend with 8 entries add moderate complexity, but the single-panel format and coherent color scheme keep it relatively readable.

This is a parallel sets visualization with three categorical variables (Class, Sex, Survived) connected by colored ribbon flows. The crossing bands between levels create moderate visual intricacy, but the chart has only 2 categories per variable and uses just two colors, keeping overall complexity moderate.

This visualization shows concentric circular rings with alternating dark green and dark red/maroon bands, likely representing a scientific field or interference pattern. While the smooth gradient transitions between rings add some visual intricacy, the overall pattern is symmetric, repetitive, and lacks any labels, axes, legends, or multi-panel complexity, keeping the cognitive demand relatively low.

This is a simple scatter-style visualization with three color-coded clusters (red, green, blue) of elongated marks inside a dashed border. There are no axes, labels, legends, or gridlines, keeping the structural complexity low. The moderate number of individual marks (~150+) with varying orientations adds some perceptual detail but overall the visual encoding is minimal.

This is a single-panel contour/heatmap plot with moderate complexity. It features multiple nested contour levels with a continuous color gradient, dual axes (k and k/k_F, E and E/E_F), overlaid boundary lines in cyan, and a detailed color legend with 12 discrete levels. The contour structure shows intricate nested patterns and sharp features along the diagonal, adding perceptual demand beyond a simple heatmap.

This is a two-panel 3D scientific visualization showing color-mapped heart surfaces with grid overlays. The right panel adds glyph markers across the surface, increasing visual detail. The color gradient encoding, wireframe mesh, and 3D perspective add moderate complexity, but the overall layout is straightforward with only two views and limited annotations.

This is a dual-line time series chart with moderately high-frequency data from 2009-2012, featuring multiple annotated callout boxes, a shaded spread area between the two lines, color-coded labels, and a descriptive subtitle. The annotations and volatile line patterns add complexity beyond a basic line chart, but it remains a single-panel visualization with only two data series.

This visualization contains a 6×9 grid of 54 small multiple heatmaps/correlation matrices, each with intricate internal patterns using purple and green color encoding. The sheer number of panels, the dense pixel-level detail within each matrix, and the need to compare patterns across multiple conditions (E5U, E5W, E1U, E1W combinations) create very high perceptual and cognitive demand.

This single-panel visualization combines multiple overlapping visual encodings: a continuous rainbow color map for displacement values, a dense vector/arrow field indicating displacement directions, and contour lines. The superposition of these three layers creates significant perceptual complexity despite being a single view, with particularly dense regions near the rod and varying arrow densities throughout.

This is a dense network graph with hundreds of nodes and numerous interconnecting edges spread across the entire canvas. Multiple clusters and subgroups are visible with varying node sizes (indicated by pink halos), and four specific subgroups are annotated with red circles. The high density of edges, overlapping structures, and distributed layout create significant perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is an extremely dense 3D visualization (likely a code city or 3D treemap) with hundreds of tightly packed rectangular blocks of varying heights rendered in grayscale. The perspective view creates heavy occlusion and visual clutter, with no labels, legends, or clear organizational hierarchy visible, making it very perceptually demanding to parse individual elements or patterns.

This is a highly intricate single-panel scientific visualization featuring a complex 3D volumetric rendering with swirling vortex structures, multiple color encodings (blue, red, orange, yellow, green), and numerous glyph-like vector elements protruding from the surface. The dense layering of visual information and rich textural detail create substantial perceptual demand, though it remains a single cohesive view rather than a multi-panel system.

This is a simple bar chart with only 3 bars, hatched fill patterns, error bars, and clear axis labels. The layout is clean and minimalistic with very few data elements, making it one of the least complex visualization types.

This is a single bar chart with approximately 20 bars showing percentage change across Latin American countries. While it has more elements than a minimal bar chart, the design is clean with a single color, clear axis labels, and straightforward layout. The rotated country labels add some visual density but the overall structure remains a standard sorted bar chart.

This is a simple histogram/bar chart showing a right-skewed distribution with approximately 15-18 bars. It uses a single color (black), has minimal labeling (though labels are partially illegible), and presents a straightforward single-variable distribution with no additional encodings or complexity layers.

This 3D flow visualization contains multiple overlapping streamlines forming vortex patterns, numerous colored directional glyphs (arrows) scattered throughout, and 3D ellipsoidal objects at vortex centers. The multi-encoding nature (color, shape, orientation, 3D depth) combined with the density of overlapping elements creates substantial perceptual complexity, though the overall layout with four repeating vortex structures provides some organizational clarity.

This is an extremely dense treemap visualization containing hundreds of nested rectangles at varying scales. The lower portion becomes increasingly granular with barely distinguishable micro-elements. While the color palette is limited (grays, greens, cyans), the sheer quantity of elements, hierarchical nesting, and the dramatic range in element sizes from large blocks to microscopic cells create very high perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is a simple dual-line chart with only two series (Total ODA and General budget support) over 11 time points. The layout is clean with clear labels, a straightforward legend, and minimal visual clutter. The added text annotations and title provide context but don't significantly increase perceptual complexity.

This is a single donut/pie chart with 6 segments, clearly labeled with percentages and a central annotation. The design is clean with a simple color palette and accompanying text. It has minimal perceptual complexity, consistent with a basic pie chart with few elements.

This image shows repeated text 'IEEE Information Visualization 2005' rendered along curved paths at decreasing sizes, with red dot markers at endpoints. It is not a traditional data visualization but rather a demonstration of text rendering on curves. The visual elements are simple and repetitive with minimal encoding complexity, though the curved text and varying sizes add some perceptual demand.

This is a stacked bar chart with approximately 30 bars, each containing three color-coded segments (Index search, Label regions, Build geometry). While it has a moderate number of data elements and uses stacked encoding which adds some perceptual demand, it remains a single-panel chart with a clear legend, standard axes, and gridlines, keeping complexity at a moderate level.

This is a stacked area chart with three categories spanning about 30 years of data. While it has moderate complexity from the overlapping stacked areas with jagged peaks and multiple color encodings, it remains a single-panel visualization with a clear legend and straightforward axes. The temporal density and layered areas push it slightly above a simple chart but it's not highly complex.

This matrix-style visualization contains 5 columns and 17 rows, creating 85 data cells encoded with three shades of blue representing high/medium/low research focus. The structured grid layout with extensive text labels on both axes and consistent color encoding creates moderate complexity, though the repetitive visual pattern and single encoding channel keep it from being highly complex.

This visualization features two side-by-side map representations of the Netherlands with numerous colored regions and numeric labels on the left, and a dense grid-based tile map on the right. The use of many distinct color encodings (10+ hues), multiple labeled regions, and two coordinated views with geographic detail creates moderate-to-high visual complexity.

This visualization shows three heatmap-style panels for different years (1999, 2003, 2005), each with 5 rows and a grid structure using a blue color gradient. While there are three panels making it a multi-panel display, the encoding is simple (single color scale), the structure is repetitive across panels, and there are minimal labels and no axes beyond row numbers. The moderate repetition and grid density push it slightly above a basic chart.

This visualization features numerous elliptical glyphs with multiple visual encodings including color intensity, size, orientation, and position, creating moderate perceptual complexity. The inclusion of a zoom/detail interaction shown via blue dashed rectangles and a color legend adds additional elements. While there is notable density and overlap in the central region, it remains a single-view visualization without multiple coordinated panels.

This visualization displays a grid of approximately 60+ 3D glyphs on a black background, each varying in orientation, size, and color (green/orange). The repetitive small-multiples layout with 3D-rendered multivariate glyphs creates moderate density, though the absence of labels, axes, or annotations and the regular grid spacing reduce cognitive load somewhat.

This is a moderately dense network/graph diagram with dozens of nodes (circles and squares) connected by numerous edges with significant edge crossings and long-distance connections. The monochrome palette reduces perceptual load somewhat, but the intricate routing of edges, hierarchical branching, and spatial spread across the canvas create substantial visual complexity.

This is a multi-component composite visualization combining a temporal stream graph at the top with two dense word clouds below, linked by interactive elements and small pie charts. The word clouds contain dozens of terms at varying sizes, the stream graph has multiple overlapping colored layers representing different topics, and there are multiple color encodings for categories (protest accounts, media, grassroot). The overall density and variety of visual elements create substantial perceptual and cognitive demand.

This timeline visualization displays 11 parallel user timelines with events encoded using multiple visual channels (color and shape) across 7 categories. The moderate density of data points, multi-encoding scheme, and the need to cross-reference the legend add cognitive demand, though the clean horizontal layout and consistent structure keep it from being highly complex.

This is a multi-layered geographic visualization with a detailed topographic relief basemap, dozens of proportional symbols encoding two variables simultaneously (color for elevation trend, size for standard error), multiple geographic boundary layers (rivers, basins, sub-regions, country borders), coordinate grid labels, statistical annotations, and a comprehensive legend. The density of overlapping symbols particularly in the western Himalaya region and the numerous text labels add significant perceptual complexity.

This phylogenetic timeline combines a branching tree structure with approximately 9 taxa, a detailed geological timescale with multiple nested periods and stages, shaded gap regions, dashed uncertainty lines, and an embedded fossil photograph. The multi-layered temporal information and taxonomic labels add moderate complexity, though the overall layout remains reasonably organized and readable.

This is a dense multi-row genomic oncoplot with dozens of patient columns and numerous feature rows spanning clinical variables, subtypes, copy number alterations, and somatic mutations across multiple pathways. It employs extensive multi-color encoding (at least 10 distinct colors) for different data types including sex, age, histology, gains, losses, and mutation categories. The combination of high data density, multiple categorical groupings (WNT, SHH, Group 3, Group 4), and a complex legend make this visualization highly demanding perceptually and cognitively.

This visualization features multiple dense network graph clusters with heavily overlapping edges, interconnected by color-coded node chains (red, orange, yellow). The detail-on-demand zoom panels add additional layers of visual information. The combination of dense edge bundles within clusters, multiple color encodings, varying node sizes, and the multi-scale representation creates substantial perceptual and cognitive demand.

This finite element mesh visualization is extremely dense with thousands of triangular elements color-mapped by a scalar field. The mesh refinement varies across the domain with particularly fine resolution near the central T-shaped cutout, creating high visual density. Though it is a single-view visualization with one color scale, the sheer number of mesh elements and their intricate geometric patterns create substantial perceptual complexity.

This thematic sound map of Jerusalem features dozens of overlapping translucent concentric circles in multiple colors encoding different sound types and their combinations, creating a dense layered visual field. The extensive legend with 16 entries, geographic base map with labeled landmarks and streets, and substantial explanatory text all add to the perceptual and cognitive load. The right portion (Old City area) is especially dense with heavily overlapping sound representations.

This is an extremely dense infographic with 100+ country rows, spanning dozens of Olympic years in columns, using multiple color encodings (pink, magenta, blue, gray). The sheer volume of small colored bars, tiny text labels, and the matrix-like layout creates very high visual complexity and cognitive demand. The dark background with numerous bright elements adds to the perceptual load.

This is an extremely dense multi-view visualization system with at least 7 coordinated panels (A through G), including a base text view, overview, page view, line view, and word view. It features multiple visual encodings including color-coded matrices, text highlights, scatter-like elements, and grid patterns. The sheer density of information, numerous labeled components, and variety of coordinated views make this among the most complex visualizations possible.

This is a dense directed graph/network visualization with hundreds of nodes and extensively crossing edges, creating significant visual clutter. The hierarchical tree-like structures combined with long-range curved connections produce a tangled, intricate layout that demands high perceptual effort to trace individual paths, though it uses only monochrome encoding and a single view.

This is a highly complex multi-component visualization combining small multiples of matrix/heatmap panels on the left, multiple word clouds in the center and enlarged on the right, and a dense network of colored flow connections linking them. The use of multiple visual encodings (color for word categories, line thickness for connection strength, text size for frequency, spatial positioning) along with overlapping elements and numerous labeled components creates significant perceptual and cognitive demand.
